title: Tennis Elbow (Lateral Epicondylitis)
license_notes: Use as source support only. Avoid verbatim copying from AAOS copyrighted patient education.
supports: Lateral epicondylitis is an overuse condition involving forearm tendon degeneration or microtearing at the outside of the elbow.
Symptoms usually develop gradually over weeks to months and often worsen with forearm activity, gripping, turning tools, or shaking hands.
X-rays can help rule out other causes of elbow pain. MRI or EMG may be used when other soft-tissue, neck, or nerve causes are suspected.
Most treatment is nonsurgical and centers on rest or decreasing painful activity, physical or occupational therapy, and gradual strengthening.
Surgery is usually considered only after persistent symptoms despite many months of nonsurgical treatment.
